figures.caption: Table 1: Main results of BrainAgent across different backbones and difficulty levels (L1–L3). TCR: Task Completion Rate; R-ACC: Routing Accuracy; TCE: Tool Call Eccuracy; Lat.: Average time cost per successful case (min). The best performance in TCR columns is marked in bold, and the second-best is underlined.

Figure 2: Efficiency–robustness trade-off on L3 tasks. BrainAgent achieves the best balance, whileSingleAgent suffers from degraded performance and higher token consumption under increasing tool overload (1×–5×).
Figure 3: Visualization of an execution trace for an L1 task.
Figure 4: Visualization of an execution trace for an L2 task.
Figure 5: Visualization of an execution trace for an L3 task.
Figure 6: A representative case of an L1 task.
Figure 7: A representative case of an L2 task.
Figure 8: A representative case of an L3 task.

Figure 9: The JSON schema for the supervisor’s decision output. The needs_dispatch flag acts as a gatekeeper, determining whether to generate a direct_response or populate the task_queue for delegation.
Table 3: Overview of the output quality evaluation cases.

Figure 10: The JSON schema for the sub-agent’s decision output. The thought field captures the reasoning trace, while the plan list contains a sequence of executable tool calls defined by their names and arguments.

Figure 11: A failure case analysis of Qwen3-Max on a Level 3 task. Although the agent correctly planned the high-level steps, it failed to execute the implicit preprocessing step (Bandpass Filter) required for Alpha band visualization, illustrating the challenge of Implicit Dependency Neglect.
